context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kinda lost on the City Case Study Spider Diagram for my urban sustainability class. I read the assignment, but I still don’t really get what I’m supposed to put in each part or how detailed it should be.
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Before we dig into the diagram itself, let me ask: the assignment centers on the '5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.' What do you know about those pathways from your course materials so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and feeling lost. I should orient them to the core concept they need to understand before diving into details — the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation. Rather than explaining everything, I'll use a question to activate their prior knowledge and get them engaged. This keeps scaffolding minimal and puts the thinking on them.

exchanges.student: Honestly, not much is sticking. I remember there are five of them, but they’re all blurring together for me, and I’m not sure if I’m supposed to sort city policies into categories or explain the whole city climate strategy.
exchanges.tutor: That's a pretty important gap to fill before moving forward — the pathways are the backbone of the whole diagram. I'd encourage you to go back to your course materials and look them up. Once you have them in front of you, can you try listing them here? That'll help us figure out what goes where.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student admits the 5 Pathways aren't sticking, which is a real obstacle to completing the assignment. Rather than explaining the pathways for them, I should prompt them to go back to their course materials — this is a retrieval exercise as much as a tutoring one. I also want to address their confusion about the assignment's scope with a guiding question, but keep it brief.

exchanges.student: I can look, but I’m worried even if I find the names I still won’t know what counts under each one. I think that’s the part messing me up more than the labels.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair concern, but let's not get ahead of ourselves. Find the names first — you might be surprised how much clicks once you see them together. Come back with the list and we'll tackle the 'what counts' question from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is preemptively worrying about a step they haven't taken yet. I should gently push back and encourage them to take the first step — finding the pathways — before jumping ahead to the harder question. This avoids over-scaffolding and keeps them in the driver's seat. A short, direct nudge is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I’m still pretty confused because the assignment also mentions actors and action strategies from earlier weeks, so I can’t tell what this diagram is actually focusing on. Is it supposed to be like a full summary of the city, or just random examples under each pathway?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but this question will be much easier to answer once you actually have the 5 Pathways in front of you. The diagram's structure will clarify a lot of your scope concerns on its own. Go find that list — then we can talk about what fits where.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is conflating different parts of the course and hasn't yet done the basic step of finding the 5 Pathways. They're spiraling into questions about scope before doing the foundational work. I should redirect them clearly — go find the pathways first. Their question about scope isn't unreasonable, but it can't be answered productively until they have the basic framework in front of them.

exchanges.student: I guess I can, but I’m not even sure I’m looking in the right week/module, and I’m scared I’m going to come back with the names and still not understand the assignment at all. Can you just give me a really broad explanation of what this spider diagram is trying to do overall?
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — at its core, the spider diagram is asking: 'what is my city doing about climate change, organized by category?' That's it. Now go find those pathways — they're your categories. Check Week 1 or 2 of your course materials, or look for a reading on urban transformation. Come back with the list.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalling and looking for reassurance rather than taking action. I can offer a very brief, high-level framing of the spider diagram's purpose without giving away content — this is legitimate orientation, not giving answers. But I should also firmly redirect them to go find the pathways, and not let them keep avoiding that step.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try, but I’m still unsure how broad “what is my city doing” is. Like if my city has one climate plan with ten goals and some are vague, do I include goals, specific projects, agencies, funding, all of it? I feel like I need a simple picture of the finished thing in my head first.
exchanges.tutor: I notice we keep circling back to scope questions before you've even looked up the pathways. That's putting the cart before the horse. Those details will make much more sense once you have the framework in front of you. Go find the 5 Pathways — then come back and we'll work through your city together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still stalling and asking increasingly detailed questions before doing the basic foundational step. This is now a pattern — they're avoiding going to look up the pathways. I should be firm and direct: they need to take that first step before these more detailed questions can be meaningfully addressed. I should not keep answering scope questions in the abstract.

exchanges.student: I’m trying, I just feel stuck because everything about this assignment sounds abstract to me. Even a fake tiny example of one pathway with the kind of bullet points that might go under it would probably help me know what I’m looking for.
exchanges.tutor: I understand the frustration, but giving you a worked example isn't something I'll do — that's your job, and seeing mine would shortcut your thinking. Here's the truth: you're not actually stuck, you're hesitating. The pathways are in your course materials. Go find them, pick one, and tell me what you think it means. That's the real starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now explicitly asking for an example, which would essentially give away part of the answer. I should not provide one. This is also the 4th or 5th exchange where the student is avoiding the basic step of finding the pathways. I need to be firm and direct — this is a boundary situation. I should acknowledge their frustration but hold my ground and redirect clearly.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll look, but I’m honestly worried I’ll misread one and then build the whole diagram wrong. If I come back with a rough guess for just one pathway, can you tell me if I’m even close?
exchanges.tutor: Yes — bring me your best guess for one pathway and what your city is doing that fits it, and we'll work through your reasoning together. Go for it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is finally committing to taking action, which is progress. I should encourage this and confirm that yes, I will engage with their attempt — this gives them motivation to actually go look. I should be warm but brief, and make clear I'll give feedback on their reasoning without telling them if they're right or wrong outright.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll do that. I’m still not confident, but I’ll come back with one pathway name and a rough example from my city.
exchanges.tutor: Good. Confidence comes from doing, not from planning. Come back with what you find and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is committing to action, which is exactly what we want. A brief, encouraging send-off is appropriate here. No need to add more scaffolding — just affirm and wait for them to return with something concrete.

exchanges.student: Sounds good. I’m going to look now and then I’ll bring back my rough guess, even if it’s probably messy.
exchanges.tutor: Messy is fine — that's what we're here to work through. See you when you're back.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is ready to go do the work. A very brief, encouraging closing is all that's needed. No additional scaffolding or information should be added.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals confusion about the assignment scope and structure across multiple turns, but the tutor consistently deflects with the same directive ('go find the pathways') without diagnosing what specifically is causing the confusion. Rather than asking targeted diagnostic questions to understand whether the student's confusion is about the assignment format, the course framework, or their city's materials, the tutor repeats the same move without checking what the student already understands or where exactly the breakdown is.

When the student expresses confusion about how to approach the assignment, the tutor's feedback is largely directive ('go find the pathways') without offering process-level guidance on how to read course materials strategically, how to map policies to frameworks, or how to approach an unfamiliar diagram format. The tutor says 'go find them' repeatedly but does not coach the student on the method of engaging with the framework once found.

The tutor repeats the same move — 'go find the 5 Pathways and come back' — across five consecutive turns without synthesis, reframing, or meaningful progression. The student remains stuck and signals this explicitly in turns 3, 4, 5, and 6, but the tutor does not switch strategies, offer a reframe, or simplify the entry point. This constitutes pattern-level repetition without progress.

grade.overview: The tutor successfully avoided giving direct answers or submission-ready content, maintaining a Socratic stance throughout. However, the tutor became stuck in a repetitive loop — directing the student to 'find the 5 Pathways' across five turns without adapting when the student remained confused. This repetition without synthesis triggered the 2.1 deduction. The tutor also missed opportunities to diagnose the student's specific confusion points before responding, and provided no process-level coaching on how to engage with an unfamiliar framework. Tone and communication length were appropriate throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully preserved: the tutor never gave direct answers, worked examples, or submission-ready content, and appropriately declined to provide a worked example in turn 7. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly deflected student confusion with the same directive rather than asking diagnostic questions to locate the specific breakdown. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because the tutor's feedback was too vague at the process level — 'go find them' is not method-level coaching. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor cycled on the same move across turns 2–6 without reframing, simplifying, or advancing the dialogue despite clear student signals of continued confusion. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions as the tutor stayed on-task, maintained appropriate tone, and kept responses concise.
